items.title: Show HN: Adrafinil – keep a lid-closed Mac awake only while agents work
items.why_it_matters: This is a tool built by someone shipping, directly solving a common problem for developers using AI agents on laptops. It prevents your MacBook from sleeping with the lid closed only when an agent (like those in Claude Code or Codex) is active, saving battery when not in use. You could install Adrafinil this week to keep your Mac awake during agent work sessions without worrying about battery drain when idle.

items.title: Show HN: Git-temp – scratchpad folder for AI agents; doesn't clutter Git status
items.why_it_matters: This is a tool built by someone shipping that directly helps with your goal of learning Git properly and maintaining commit discipline. It provides a temporary folder for AI agent scratchpad files that Git ignores, keeping your repository clean. You could integrate Git-temp into your current project's Git workflow this week to prevent temporary agent files from cluttering your commit history.

items.title: How Boris Cherny Uses Claude Code
items.why_it_matters: This resource shows how an experienced developer uses Claude Code, your primary coding AI. Learning how others direct AI for coding can help you improve your own 'direction' skills and potentially save costs, similar to the high-rated item about using Claude Code with other models. You could read through Boris's workflow this week and try to apply one of his specific prompting techniques or delegation strategies in your next Claude Code session.

items.title: Show HN: AI-whisper – Claude works better when Codex watches its back
items.why_it_matters: This is a tool built by someone shipping that offers a specific technique to improve how you use Claude and Codex together, both tools you already use. It suggests that having Codex monitor Claude's output can lead to better results. You could try integrating this 'Codex watches Claude's back' technique into your next coding session this week to see if it improves the quality of your AI-generated code.

items.title: Show HN: Peek-CLI: let coding agents see your browser
items.why_it_matters: This is a tool built by someone shipping that allows your AI coding agents to 'see' your browser, giving them more context about what you're working on. This could make your 'direction' tasks easier by providing agents with visual information. You could experiment with Peek-CLI this week to see if giving your agents browser context improves their ability to understand and execute your coding requests.

items.title: Higher rate limits on the Claude API
items.why_it_matters: Anthropic has increased rate limits for the Claude API, which you use for your projects. This means you can make more requests to the API in a given timeframe, reducing potential bottlenecks for more complex or intensive agent applications. You could review the new rate limits this week and consider if any of your planned project features or agent workflows that might have previously hit limits are now more feasible.

items.title: Show HN: Claude-CLI – Run Claude Code in a throwaway Docker container
items.why_it_matters: This is a tool built by someone shipping that lets you run Claude Code within an isolated Docker container. This can be useful for maintaining a clean development environment, testing code without affecting your local setup, or ensuring reproducibility. You could try using Claude-CLI this week to run a specific Claude Code task in an isolated environment, especially if you're experimenting with new dependencies or configurations.

items.title: Show HN: Open Tag, the open source Claude Tag
items.why_it_matters: This is an open-source tool built by someone shipping that provides a 'Claude Tag' implementation. While the exact functionality isn't detailed, an open-source version of a Claude-related tool could offer more flexibility or insights into how to integrate Claude features into your Next.js projects. You could explore Open Tag this week to see if it offers a more flexible way to interact with Claude in your projects or to learn how a specific Claude feature is implemented.

items.title: Corv: An SSH client for AI agents (and humans)
items.why_it_matters: This is a tool built by someone shipping that provides an SSH client specifically designed for AI agents (and humans). Giving your agents SSH capabilities could open up new possibilities for remote execution, deployment, or interacting with your Supabase backend or Vercel deployments. You could experiment with Corv this week to enable an agent to interact with a remote server (e.g., a Vercel deployment or Supabase instance) for deployment or monitoring tasks.

items.title: Enki – memory for AI agents that keeps ~half as much and answers as well
items.why_it_matters: This is a tool that focuses on efficient memory management for AI agents, claiming to maintain performance while using less memory. For your goal of directing AI, optimizing agent memory could lead to more efficient and potentially cheaper operations. You could explore Enki this week to see if integrating it into an agent project improves memory efficiency without sacrificing response quality.
